text: The surgery begins with a classical posterior lateral thoracotomy and then we raise an intercostal muscle flap
in case we need to use it to wrap around the anastomosis.
Here you can see the pleura being incised at the junction of the azygous vein and the superior vena cava.
Then we approach the right main bronchus anteriorly and clear all the soft tissues and the lymph nodes surrounding it.
Just to orient our viewers to the anatomy, here we are dissecting on the anterior surface of the right main bronchus in the infra-azygous region.
We try and preserve the vagus nerve if possible by separating it out from the right main bronchus.
This is followed by looping of the right main bronchus.
Once the right main bronchus has been looped, before we start the resection, we take a few stage sutures.
Now, we move on to the resectional part of the surgery.
where we are dividing the right main bronchus.
This is a crucial part of the surgery where we have to ensure negative margins.
The margins are then sent for frozen section analysis and we try to preserve as much of the posterior wall as possible.
It is our policy to perform a formal lymphadenectomy in all cases of carcinoid tumours.
Coming to the anastomosis part of the surgery,
The principles of airway anastomosis remain the same as other anastomosis in other parts of the body.
The principles are to achieve a tension-free closure and an airtight closure.
To do this, we usually use a continuous or a running suture technique.
We use a non-absorbable suture such as PDF or ProLeaf.
We generally prefer to use a 4-0 suture for this anastomosis.
We start off usually with the posterior wall of the bronchus after taking adequate amount of stress sutures to bring the two ends together.
There are various ways to do this anastomosis.
Some surgeons prefer to use an interrupted technique which is also acceptable.
Like in any other anastomosis, blood supply is important to ensure adequate healing of the anastomosis.
The bronchial tree is usually supplied by the bronchial arteries which are a direct branch of the aorta and the supply is through the lateral wall
of the bronchus and hence while mobilizing the bronchus or doing a lymphadenectomy, the surgeon should not be too aggressive in his approach.
If possible, the surgeon should try and preserve the branches of the bronchial artery in this region.
Some salient points need to be kept in mind while doing this anastomosis.
It is necessary to ensure an uniform tension
This is done by placing equidistant sutures and also by using a nerve hook as shown in the pictures to ensure that the sutures are adequately tightened.
It is also important to ensure the orientation of the anastomosis.
The cartilaginous part of one end should be anastomosed to the cartilaginous part of the other and the same should be followed for the muscular part.
Once the anastomosis is completed, an air leak test is done to ensure the air tightness of the anastomosis.
An intercostal muscle flap can be placed to reinforce the anastomosis.
This patient had an uneventful post-operative course and his HPR was suggestible for typical carcinoid tumor.

In this intraoperative illustration, we would like to orient our viewers to the location of the tumors which is located between the junction of the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermediate.
Here we join the surgery after looping the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius.
We go on and divide them sharply.
Once the specimen has been resected, we send it for frozen section analysis.
For carcinoid tumors, a microscopic negative margin is sufficient.
Here you can see the bronchus intermedius being divided followed by division of the right main bronchus.
First anastomosis is between the bronchus intermedius and the right main bronchus.
As previously elucidated, the principles of right-way anastomosis remain the same.
That is to achieve an adequate tension-free tight closure and also a healthy anastomosis by ensuring adequate blood supply to the anastomotic region.

There are many factors to consider before designing on the configuration of this anastomosis.
One of them is the size disparity between the right upper lobe bronchus.
and the bronchus intermediates in the right main bronchus.
The lumen of the bronchus intermediates in the right main bronchus were comparable in size and hence we decided to go with this configuration.
Reanastomosing the upper lobe at a point higher up in the right main bronchus had two reasons.
One, we thought it would be more physiological and would mimic the natural anatomy more closely than if we had
created a trifurcation point and a new secondary carina on the right side.
And secondly, this would take care of the size disparity between the right upper lobe and the right main bronchus.
As you can see, a new ostia is created for the right upper lobe at a point closer to the carina on the right main bronchus.
This anastomosis, because it involves smaller sizes of lumen, is more challenging than the anastomosis shown earlier.
The technique of an anastomosis, however, remains the same.
We have used a continuous suturing technique using PDS 4-0.

The objective of using these two cases in the same presentation was to show that in spite of having relatively large size tumor, we were able to completely preserve all the three lobes of the lump using apparent time of preserving or a bronchoplasty technique.
The immediate post-op bronchoscopy showed all the lumens to be patent, including that of the right upper lobe bronchus.
And then a post-operative reformatted CT scan confirmed the same finding.
